figures.caption: Table 2: Main results over three runs. Best and second-best per column; ∆ is SERO’s gain on the row average. 5 / 5 mark the top-1 and top-2 method per backbone by average score. Full results are provided in Table 7.

Table 8: Role lifecycle statistics. Gemini denotes Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, GPT denotes GPT-4o-mini, Qwen denotes Qwen3-8b, and NP/OB/TB denote NATURALPLAN, OLYMPIADBENCH, and TABLEBENCH.

Figure 1: Three role-pool paradigms for multi-agent LLM reasoning. Left: a frozen pool preserves the five contracts (capability, communication, validation, aggregation, output protocol) but admits no new roles. Middle: unconstrained editing breaks one or more. Right: SERO gates each edit, committing only those preserving all five.
Figure 3: Hyper-parameter sensitivity of SERO under one-at-a-time sweeps. Scores are task accuracy; the NATURALPLAN curve uses partial accuracy. Stars mark the best score per benchmark.
Table 7: Seed-level results by backbone. P and E denote NATURALPLAN partial and exact accuracy. Best and second-best per column within each backbone block.
Figure 14: NATURALPLAN seed pool (part 2/6): trip flight route planner and trip day logistics formatter.

Table 1: LLM multi-agent systems on six design dimensions: Inventory Adaptation (role set adapts without redeployment), Topology Adaptation (per-task communication graph), Edit Ops (Add/Remove; ◦ also covers selection, pruning, or prompt rewrite without persistent role-card edits), Cross-Task Persistence (changes persist), Role Typing (typed objects vs prompts), Verification Loop (output-acceptance signal). ✓ supports, ◦ partial, × absent.
Table 10: Credit and DAG alignment statistics. Model and benchmark abbreviations follow Table 8.
Figure 9: Static DAG MAS baseline for NATURALPLAN (part 1/4): topology and task-and-contract parser.
Figure 15: NATURALPLAN seed pool (part 3/6): calendar parser and calendar slot checker.
Figure 4: Dominant family-level active-set signatures of SERO on OLYMPIADBENCH. Each row is a frequent multiset of role families; the bar reports its share among evaluation instances.

Figure 2: Framework overview of SERO. 1⃝ Shared inference operator F retrieves an active team, runs a creditranked DAG ending at a locked aggregator with optional validator repair, updating credit via fast, leave-one-out, and EMA signals. 2⃝ A typed role pool stores cards (capability, protocol, type, credit, protection), with a locked validator and aggregator anchoring the five contract rails. 3⃝ Controller πθ proposes an ADD/REMOVE/NOOP edit, generator E builds a candidate pool, F re-scores it for reward Rt, and Γ commits or rolls back.
Figure 11: Static DAG MAS baseline for NATURALPLAN (part 3/4): meeting specialist and candidate synthesizer.
Figure 10: Static DAG MAS baseline for NATURALPLAN (part 2/4): trip and calendar specialists.
Figure 8: Workflow baseline for NATURALPLAN (part 3/3): validator and finalizer.
Table 6: SERO hyperparameter profiles for the three backbones. †For Gemini-2.5-flash-lite on NATURALPLAN, µ = 0.2 instead of 0.05.
Table 9: Evaluation-time active-set diversity. Model and benchmark abbreviations follow Table 8.
Figure 23: Task-conditioned specialist-role activation on OLYMPIADBENCH with Qwen3-8B (seed 44), in the same format as Fig. 22. Mean inter-group gap is 0.346.

Figure 16: NATURALPLAN seed pool (part 4/6): meeting window analyzer and meeting route scheduler.

Figure 22: Task-conditioned specialist-role activation on OLYMPIADBENCH with GPT-4o-mini (seed 42). Rows are subject groups, columns are specialist roles, and each cell gives the fraction of in-group instances that activate the role. Mean inter-group gap is 0.206.

Figure 21: Role lifecycle of a SERO trajectory on OLYMPIADBENCH with Gemini-2.5-flash-lite (seed 42). Top: controller operation per training episode, with ✓ and × marking committed and rejected edits. Middle: lifecycle state of every persistent role across episodes, colored as absent, in pool, or active. Bottom: family-level composition of active roles per episode.
